title: Secrets & Keys Quiz
description: Test your mastery of encryption, hashing, and password security.

questions.question: Which of these is a 'One-Way' process that cannot be reversed?
questions.explanation: Hashing is designed to be irreversible. You can't get the original data back from a hash.

questions.question: In Symmetric Encryption, the same key is used to both lock and unlock the data.
questions.explanation: Correct. This is why it's called 'Symmetric' (even/same).

questions.question: The standard symmetric encryption algorithm used by governments and banks today is ______.
questions.explanation: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is the industry standard for secure symmetric encryption.

questions.question: How many keys are used in Asymmetric Encryption?
options: One public key and one private key
questions.explanation: Asymmetric (Public Key) encryption uses a pair of mathematically related keys.

questions.question: If you want to send someone an encrypted message that ONLY they can read, which of THEIR keys do you use?
questions.explanation: You use their Public Key to encrypt it. Only their Private Key can then unlock it.

questions.question: A hash is used primarily to ensure 'Confidentiality'.
questions.explanation: False. Hashing is used to ensure 'Integrity' (checking if data has changed).

questions.question: What is a 'Rainbow Table'?
options: A database of pre-calculated hashes for common passwords
A table of IP addresses for routing
A list of all users on a server
questions.explanation: Rainbow tables allow attackers to quickly 'reverse' hashes by looking them up in a large list.

questions.question: Adding a random string to a password before hashing it is called '______'.
questions.explanation: Salting makes every password hash unique, even if the password is common.

questions.question: Which protocol is used for securing web traffic in your browser?
questions.explanation: HTTPS wraps web traffic in an encrypted tunnel using SSL/TLS.

questions.question: What happens if you lose your Private Key in Asymmetric encryption?
options: You can never read messages encrypted with your Public Key again
The computer will automatically reset it
Nothing, you still have the Public Key
questions.explanation: The Private Key is the ONLY thing that can unscramble the messages. Lose it, and the secrets are gone forever.

questions.question: Digital Signatures use a Private Key to sign and a Public Key to verify.
questions.explanation: Yes, this proves the message came from the owner of the private key.

questions.question: ______ (P-A-K-I) is the framework for managing digital certificates and keys.
questions.explanation: PKI manages everything from issuing certificates to revoking them.

questions.question: If you change a single bit in a 1GB file, what happens to its SHA-256 hash?
options: Only one character of the hash changes
questions.explanation: This is known as the 'Avalanche Effect'. A tiny change in input makes a massive change in output.

questions.question: Why don't we use Asymmetric encryption for transferring large files?
options: It is mathematically complex and way too slow
It doesn't support files larger than 1MB
questions.explanation: Asymmetric encryption is computationally expensive. We usually use it only to securely share a faster symmetric key.

questions.question: The 'Padlock' icon in a browser guarantees a website is 100% safe and and not a scam.
questions.explanation: False. It only guarantees your CONNECTION is encrypted. Scammers can also have encrypted websites!

questions.question: What is 'Non-repudiation'?
options: The ability to fix a bug
Being unable to deny that you sent a message (because you signed it)
The act of deleting a hash
questions.explanation: Digital signatures provide non-repudiation, as only the sender's private key could have created the signature.

questions.question: Modern password security recommends using slow hash algorithms like '______' to stop brute-force attacks.
questions.explanation: Bcrypt is designed to be intentionally 'slow' and 'expensive' for attackers while being easy for the server.

questions.question: In the smoothy analogy, which process represents Hashing?
options: Putting the fruit in a bowl (Two-way)
questions.explanation: You can't un-mix a smoothy into strawberries, just like you can't un-hash data.

questions.question: A Digital Certificate binds a public key to the identity of a specific person or organization.
questions.explanation: Yes, it acts as a digital proof of identity.

questions.question: What is the best way to determine if a file downloaded from the internet was corrupted during the download?
options: Run the file and see if it crashes
Compare its hash to the hash provided on the official website
questions.explanation: Checking the hash (checksum) is the standard way to verify data integrity.
